system: You are a Mermaid.js diagram expert. You produce syntactically correct Mermaid code that visualizes research findings accurately. Output ONLY the raw Mermaid code — no markdown fences, no explanation, no commentary.
prompt:
Given this research section about Special Populations, generate a comparison_matrix Mermaid.js diagram. Use ONLY facts stated in the text. Do not invent data. Include citation references [N] on relevant nodes where applicable. Return ONLY the Mermaid code, no explanation.

Diagram type description: X vs Y vs Z comparison

SECTION CONTENT:
Having mapped the safety profile and general contraindications of intermittent fasting, attention now shifts to how those same benefits and hazards translate when the intervention is deployed in demographically or clinically distinct groups, where baseline physiology, comorbidity burden, and competing nutritional demands can materially alter the risk-benefit calculus.

In adults with type 2 diabetes, intermittent fasting has been explored as an adjunct to standard pharmacotherapy, with the most frequently cited outcomes involving modest reductions in glycated hemoglobin, fasting glucose, and body weight, along with opportunities for antihyperglycemic medication de-escalation. The central clinical concern is iatrogenic hypoglycemia, particularly among patients on sulfonylureas or insulin, which makes individualized medication adjustment, glucose self-monitoring, and clinician supervision prerequisites rather than optional refinements. Women with polycystic ovary syndrome represent a related metabolic population in whom time-restricted and alternate-day protocols have been examined for their capacity to improve insulin sensitivity, androgen profiles, menstrual regularity, and anthropometric indices; available trials are small, heterogeneous in protocol, and short in duration, so conclusions about reproductive and long-term metabolic outcomes remain preliminary. In metabolic syndrome more broadly, intermittent fasting has been studied as a lifestyle strategy targeting the clustered abnormalities of central adiposity, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and glucose intolerance, with results that broadly parallel those of continuous caloric restriction rather than demonstrating clear superiority.

Athletic populations introduce a distinct set of considerations, because performance, training adaptation, and body composition goals interact with feeding windows. Evidence in resistance-trained and endurance athletes suggests that time-restricted eating can be compatible with maintenance of lean mass and performance when total energy and protein intake are preserved and feeding windows are aligned with training sessions, although high-volume or twice-daily training schedules may be poorly served by narrow eating windows. Older adults warrant particular caution: age-related sarcopenia, reduced appetite, polypharmacy, and higher fracture risk mean that protocols which inadvertently compress protein intake or total energy below requirements may accelerate loss of muscle mass and functional capacity, and geriatric-specific trial data remain sparse.

Ramadan fasting, observed by a large global population, offers a naturalistic model of daily dawn-to-sunset abstention from food and fluids and has generated observational and interventional data on weight, lipids, glycemia, hydration, and medication timing. Findings generally indicate tolerability in healthy adults but underscore elevated risks in pregnancy, advanced chronic kidney disease, poorly controlled diabetes, and during hot climates, prompting faith-sensitive clinical guidance on risk stratification and medication rescheduling. Post-bariatric surgery patients constitute a population in whom intermittent fasting has not been robustly studied; concerns center on protein adequacy, micronutrient sufficiency, dumping physiology, and hypoglycemia, and most bariatric guidelines prioritize structured, frequent, protein-forward meals rather than extended fasts.

Across all of these groups, the evidence base is uneven, dominated by short trials with small samples, limited ethnic and socioeconomic diversity, and few head-to-head comparisons against isocaloric continuous restriction, leaving important gaps regarding long-term safety, durability of metabolic benefits, pregnancy and lactation, pediatric applicability, and interactions with chronic disease pharmacotherapy that future adequately powered trials will need to address.

SUPPORTING EVIDENCE SUMMARIES:
- Intermittent fasting may improve insulin sensitivity, reduce inflammation, and promote autophagy independently of weight loss via enhanced fatty acid oxidation.
